topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark intrusion into the quiet afternoon. My stomach clenched instantly, a familiar, tight knot forming beneath my ribs. It was a request. From Mark. The name alone sent a cold wave washing over me, making the air feel thin. I stared at the profile picture—a smug grin I remembered too well—and my fingers hovered over the 'Decline' button, trembling slightly. Why now? After all this time, after everything I’d built since those relentless years, he resurfaced. A slow burn of resentment started to rise, hot and prickly against my skin. I closed my eyes, trying to steady my breathing, but the silence of my room amplified the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I read the message twice. It was short, almost dismissive, laced with that familiar, irritating bravado. A slow, involuntary shiver traced down my spine as I processed the connection. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles strain in my neck. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and looked out the window at the busy street below. Every nerve ending felt exposed, buzzing with a strange, unwelcome energy. I picked up my phone again, my thumb hovering over the acceptance button, before finally letting the device drop onto the desk with a soft thud. The residue of old antagonism clung to the space between us, heavy and suffocating.

[story 3]

When I saw the incoming request, a sudden, sharp intake of breath caught in my throat. My hands, resting on the keyboard, began to shake almost imperceptibly. It wasn't anger that surfaced immediately; it was a deep, sinking feeling, like being pulled underwater. I took a long, deliberate sip of water, the coolness doing nothing to soothe the internal tension coiling within me. I scrolled through my memories of him—the sharp words, the isolating glances—and felt a profound sense of vulnerability. My shoulders slumped, a physical manifestation of the weight settling onto my frame. I didn't move to respond. Instead, I simply let the notification sit there, a tiny, unwelcome stone in the calm of my evening.
